success_surface: Successfully created dynamic lookup formulas with proper workbook inspection, formula construction, error recovery, and validation
effective_behaviors: Perform comprehensive structural analysis before implementing solutions
Use iterative formula refinement with simpler fallbacks when complexity causes issues
Print diagnostic intermediate values during execution for validation
Apply both static values and formulas for robustness during testing

Choose appropriate libraries (openpyxl over pandas) when preserving formulas is required
Handle library-specific API errors with recovery strategies
evidence: Agent loaded workbook and inspected max_row, max_col, headers, and column mappings before writing code

memory_items.title: Structural Inspection Before Implementation
memory_items.lesson: Always inspect workbook schema (headers, dimensions, data types) before manipulating cells or creating formulas
memory_items.generalization: When loading any spreadsheet for manipulation, first verify max_row/max_col, check header row integrity, confirm target cell locations, and map relevant column relationships. This prevents out-of-bounds errors and ensures correct formula references.

memory_items.title: Formula Construction with Iterative Refinement
memory_items.lesson: Build formulas starting with simpler logic; iterate toward complexity only if necessary
memory_items.generalization: For dynamic spreadsheet formulas (especially array/filters), begin with core logic patterns like INDEX+SMALL+IF or similar combinations. Test each iteration, simplify if errors occur, and prioritize compatibility across Excel versions.

memory_items.title: Diagnostic Logging for Execution Validation
memory_items.lesson: Print intermediate values during code execution to validate assumptions and catch mismatches early
memory_items.generalization: When generating Python code for spreadsheet operations, include print statements for critical variables (lookup values, matched results, row indices). This provides immediate feedback and simplifies debugging when formula results don't match expectations.

memory_items.title: Dual Output Strategy for Formula Cells
memory_items.lesson: Write both calculated values and formulas to ensure results are visible regardless of calculation context
memory_items.generalization: For cells requiring formulas, consider populating them with both the formula string AND pre-calculated values where applicable. This ensures visual correctness during testing while maintaining the dynamic capability for end users.

memory_items.title: OpenPyXL Over Pandas for Formula Preservation
memory_items.lesson: Use openpyxl when you need to write or preserve Excel formulas; pandas converts formulas to static values
memory_items.generalization: When the task involves creating formulas, referencing cell addresses dynamically, or preserving existing formula strings, load and save via openpyxl Workbook objects rather than pandas read_excel/to_excel workflows.

memory_items.title: Scope-Constrained Manipulation
memory_items.lesson: Limit all modifications strictly to the answer_position range specified in instructions
memory_items.generalization: Before writing any changes, define the exact cell range boundaries from task parameters. Clear or modify only those cells to avoid unintended side effects on unrelated worksheet data.

edits.content:
## Trace-Derived Lessons

### Structural Analysis First
Before implementing spreadsheet manipulations, always inspect the workbook's complete structure: check max_row/max_col, verify header integrity, and map column relationships. Use openpyxl to examine raw cell values and positions before applying transformations.

### Formula Development Strategy
- Start with simple, proven formula patterns (INDEX+SMALL+IF, etc.)
- Iterate toward complexity only when required
- Write both formula strings and pre-calculated values for testing robustness
- Ensure array formulas are compatible with target Excel version

### Execution Validation
- Print intermediate diagnostic values (lookups, matches, row indices)
- Confirm cell contents match expectations before finalizing output
- Handle library-specific API errors gracefully without aborting entire operation

### Library Selection Guidelines
- **pandas**: For bulk data reading/writing where formulas become static values
- **openpyxl**: Required when formulas must be preserved, created, or referenced dynamically

### Scope Discipline
- Always define answer_position boundaries before editing
- Limit modifications strictly to specified ranges
- Clear existing content in target cells before applying new values/formulas
